sections.content: Maritime radio communications follow a strict priority hierarchy. Higher-priority traffic always takes precedence, and all other stations must maintain silence when distress traffic is in progress. Understanding this hierarchy is fundamental to safe radio operation.

rows: Vessel sinking, fire on board, crew in immediate danger
Person overboard, engine failure, medical emergency
Port operations, berth requests, supply orders

sections.content: When you hear a distress signal, you must immediately cease all non-essential transmissions. Urgency traffic takes priority over safety and routine, and safety traffic takes priority over routine communications.

sections.prompt: Which category of communication has the highest priority?
sections.explanation: Distress has the highest priority. It indicates grave and imminent danger to a vessel or person. All other traffic must yield to distress communications.
